summary.title: PROVO - Privacy-Enhanced Resort and Vacancy Online
summary.description: DFD for the Privacy-Enhanced Resort and Vacancy Online system with STRIDE threat analysis. TDT4237 Exercise 3.

diagrams.title: Stride Diagram 1
diagrams.placeholder: PROVO system DFD with STRIDE threats

threats.title: Guest Impersonation via Stolen Credentials
threats.description: An attacker obtains stolen credentials (e.g., via phishing) and authenticates as a legitimate guest or staff member through the Auth Gateway, gaining unauthorized access to booking and guest data.
threats.mitigation: Enforce MFA for all users. Rate-limit login attempts. Use anomaly detection on login patterns. Integrate breach-credential checking.

threats.title: JWT Token Forgery from Compromised IdP
threats.description: If the External IdP is compromised or its signing keys are leaked, an attacker can forge JWT tokens to impersonate any user, including admins, bypassing the Auth Gateway entirely.
threats.mitigation: Validate JWT audience and scope claims strictly. Rotate IdP signing keys regularly. Monitor for token anomalies. Implement short-lived tokens with refresh rotation.

threats.title: Unauthorized Modification of Booking Records
threats.description: A malicious or compromised staff member could alter booking records in the Booking Service (e.g., changing guest identity links, billing data) without authorization, violating data integrity.
threats.mitigation: Implement role-based access control (RBAC) with least privilege. Enforce audit logging for all write operations. Use database-level integrity constraints and signed records.

threats.title: Repudiation of Administrative Booking Actions
threats.description: Staff members could perform privileged actions (e.g., deleting bookings, modifying PII) and later deny them, since the Booking Service may lack comprehensive audit trails.
threats.mitigation: Implement tamper-evident audit logging for all booking operations. Log user identity, timestamp, action type, and affected records. Store logs in an append-only system.

threats.title: Elevation of Privilege via IDOR in Booking API
threats.description: A low-privileged staff user (e.g., concierge) exploits missing object-level authorization checks (IDOR) to access or modify bookings belonging to other guests or access billing PII they should not see.
threats.mitigation: Enforce object-level authorization checks on every API endpoint. Validate that the requesting user has explicit permission for the accessed resource. Apply OWASP API Security Top 10 mitigations.

threats.title: Denial of Service Attack on Media Linker API
threats.description: An attacker floods the Media Linker API with requests, exhausting processing resources or storage I/O, making surveillance functionality unavailable and potentially causing security blind spots.
threats.mitigation: Implement rate limiting and API throttling. Deploy the service behind a WAF. Use circuit breakers and auto-scaling. Set up availability monitoring and alerting.

threats.title: Unauthorized Cross-Database Identity Correlation via Media Linker
threats.description: A low-privileged authenticated user exploits insufficient scope validation on JWTs accepted by the Media Linker API to link media subject IDs with real guest identities, bypassing the pseudonymization boundary and deanonymizing high-profile guests.
threats.mitigation: Enforce strict JWT audience and scope validation. Apply RBAC: only authorized roles (e.g., security admin) may access identity-media linkages. Separate the resolution capability into a privileged microservice with extra authentication.

threats.title: Inference Attack on Deterministic Pseudonymization Keys
threats.description: If pseudonymization uses deterministic, reversible keys (e.g., HMAC with a static key), an attacker with read access to both the pseudonymized DB and identity DB can correlate records and re-identify guests.
threats.mitigation: Use cryptographically random pseudonyms stored in a separate mapping table with strict access controls. Avoid deterministic derivation. Apply tokenization rather than format-preserving encryption.

threats.title: Adversarial Attack on Face Recognition Model to Spoof Identity
threats.description: An attacker uses adversarial inputs (e.g., printed adversarial patches, IR-modified appearance) to fool the AI face recognition system into misidentifying an unauthorized intruder as a legitimate guest or staff member, granting physical access.
threats.mitigation: Use liveness detection alongside face recognition. Implement multi-factor physical verification. Monitor for unusual recognition confidence scores. Regularly test the model against adversarial examples. Use diverse camera angles.

threats.title: Biometric Data Leakage from Face Recognition Model or Training Data
threats.description: The AI face recognition service stores facial feature embeddings (biometric data) that, if exfiltrated, can permanently compromise guests' biometric privacy. Model inversion attacks could also reconstruct facial images from embeddings.
threats.mitigation: Encrypt biometric embeddings at rest and in transit. Apply differential privacy techniques during training. Limit retention of raw facial embeddings. Conduct regular access audits. Comply with biometric data regulations.

threats.title: Exfiltration of Guest PII from Identity Database
threats.description: An attacker who compromises the Guest Identity DB gains access to real names, billing details, passport data, and PII of high-profile guests (celebrities, politicians), enabling blackmail, extortion, or targeted attacks.
threats.mitigation: Encrypt the database at rest using AES-256. Apply column-level encryption for PII fields. Enforce network isolation (no direct internet access). Use privileged access management (PAM). Enable database activity monitoring.

threats.title: Tampering with Surveillance Footage in Media Database
threats.description: A malicious insider or attacker with DB access could delete, modify or replace surveillance footage, either covering tracks after an incident or planting false evidence.
threats.mitigation: Implement cryptographic hashing (SHA-256) of footage files upon ingest. Store hashes in a separate, write-once log. Enforce WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage policy for surveillance data.
